title: Call for action as bed block soars 60pc
full_text:
Hospital bed block has soared by 60 per cent in the past three years, new analysis has revealed, sparking calls for Anthony Albanese to fund 600 “transition” beds or risk exacerbating an issue already costing the health system up to $1.6bn a year.
As the Coalition accuses Labor of demonstrating a worrying “pattern of behaviour” in its treatment of older Australians, the biggest non-government health and aged-care provider is demanding immediate commonwealth investment into a system in crisis. Catholic Health Australia has released data showing that the number of days patients were kept in hospital despite being ready for discharge into aged care increased from 286,050 in 2020-21 to 460,122 in 2023-24.
The figures come ahead of a roundtable between the leading provider and government officials, including Aged Care Minister Sam Rae, who will likely be pushed to release 60,000 extra homecare packages a year and open up 5000 additional places in residential aged care.
However, given the budget and workforce constraints facing the commonwealth, CHA also proposed a short-term solution and called for Labor to fund 600 “transition beds” that would offer patients short-term, non-acute care. “The 600 beds give patients somewhere to go while they wait for a permanent place. They take the immediate pressure off hospitals and, because they’re linked into aged care and community support, patients keep moving through to the right setting instead of getting stuck again,” CHA acting chief executive Katharine Bassett said.
CHA’s plan also includes a proposal for 150 hospital liaison officers to be placed across the health, aged-care, disability and housing systems, with the authority to move patients through the systems as needed.
Dr Bassett said there were already such liaison officers dedicated to moving NDIS participants out of hospitals, saving millions of dollars in health system costs each year.
However, disability advocates have raised concern that efforts to ensure participants don’t languish in hospital risk being undermined by the government’s latest NDIS reforms, hoped to save $185bn over the decade.
Following a suite of amendments to the NDIS bill passing on Wednesday, including a requirement that the NDIS Minister table all pricing advice before using the new powers bestowed on him by the legislation to make unilateral decisions, the lower house is expected to pass the reforms on Thursday.
Scrutiny on the government’s handling of NDIS, aged care and health systems ramped up on Wednesday, as the Coalition vowed to oppose Labor’s plan to scrap private health insurance rebates for over 65s.
Opposition health spokeswoman Anne Ruston said it was increasingly clear the government was making decisions driven first and foremost by “the budget bottom line”. “It’s a pattern of behaviour by the government that is really disrespecting older Australians when we believe that they deserve our respect and a dignified ageing process,” she told The Australian.
In introducing the Modernising the Private Health Insurance Rebate Bill last week, Health and Ageing Minister Mark Butler said the average premium increase for the approximately 3.2 million impacted Australians aged over 65 would be $250 annually, or less than $1 per day.
“This is a relatively low change in participation compared with the volume of services in hospitals, which means the impact on hospital admissions is expected to be marginal,” Mr Butler said. The Coalition’s rejection of the health rebate changes, which pre-empt a Senate inquiry scrutinising the policy that isn’t due to report until October, means it will be left to the Greens to either push through or vote down the bill, with sources in revealing MPs were so far undecided on the policy. “If there was one issue the people are contacting us about, this is it. The security of knowing you’ve got access to healthcare would be one of the greatest priorities for older Australians and I think the government exploiting that is pretty callous,” Senator Ruston said.
